context: The overly righteous are like the Pharisee who "went up to the temple to pray" (Lk 18:10), and the overly wise may be likened to the wise of this world ([[1CO.1:18]]). St. Paul writes, "Therefore let him who thinks he stands take heed lest he fall" ([[1CO.10:12]]).
The threefold cord may be linked to the virtues of faith, hope, and love ([[1CO.13:13]]).

The overly righteous are like the Pharisee who "went up to the temple to pray" (Lk 18:10), and the overly wise may be likened to the wise of this world ([[1CO.1:18]]). St. Paul writes, "Therefore let him who thinks he stands take heed lest he fall" ([[1CO.10:12]]).

Even the human quest for knowledge and wisdom proves fruitless, because man cannot remember what he learned. Nothing new (v. 9) contrasts with the OT prophets who proclaim a new covenant ([[JER.38:31]]; Is 65:27) created in Christ.

We labor that we may profit, but labor loses its meaning (see [[MAT.16:26]]) when it has no eternal purpose. From generation to generation, we inherit death ([[JOB.14:10]]). But Christ, the Sun of Righteousness (Mal 4:2), gives meaning to our labor and raises us up from death to life.

Job expressed a similar sentiment of being oppressed when he wished that he had never been born to see such sorrow ([[JOB.3:10]]-16). But Christ has sent a comforter, the Holy Spirit ([[JOH.14:16]]), to wipe away all tears. Verse 2 speaks of the communion of the saints.

Earthly food and drink still ends in death, but the eternal food of the Eucharist ([[JOH.6:55]]) imparts good--that is, wisdom, knowledge, and gladness (v. 26)--to those who labor for God.

Life is no more than a shadow (v. 12). No matter what we achieve on this earth, we cannot take it with us when we die. At the same time, the Christian may rejoice, for true Wisdom is Christ. The soul that labors for the food of immortality is covered not in darkness but in light, and lives not a thousand years twice (v. 6), but forever ([[JOH.6:55]]-58).

The living dog is an image of the Gentile believers ([[MAT.15:26]], [[MAT.15:27]]), and the dead lion images the nation of Israel.
The living dog is an image of the Gentile believers ([[MAT.15:26]], [[MAT.15:27]]), and the dead lion images the nation of Israel.

We labor that we may profit, but labor loses its meaning (see [[MAT.16:26]]) when it has no eternal purpose. From generation to generation, we inherit death ([[JOB.14:10]]). But Christ, the Sun of Righteousness (Mal 4:2), gives meaning to our labor and raises us up from death to life.

Christ's "face shone like the sun" on Mount Tabor ([[MAT.17:2]]). The faithful also shine with His ineffable light, as Christ Himself said, "You are the light of the world" ([[MAT.5:14]]). Therefore, let us work to acquire the wisdom that is Christ our king, that we might shine as lights "in the midst of a crooked and perverse generation" ([[PHP.2:15]]).

The city can be seen as an image of the Kingdom of heaven, ruled by Christ the king and His princes (v. 16), the faithful. Christ calls the scribes and Pharisees fools (v. 15) for being too blind to see the truth ([[MAT.23:17]], [[MAT.23:19]]).

The eternal Word--the beginning and the end (v. 11; [[REV.22:13]])--came into time and redeemed all things. He turned our weeping into joy, our mourning into dancing, our hatred into love, and our time into eternity.
All, that is, everything in life that is apart from the purpose for which God created us, is vanity (see [[ROM.8:20]]).
